entry.title: The Neuma Grand Residency
entry.summary: As of the close of 2025, the global hotel construction pipeline reached…
entry.content:
As of the close of 2025, the global hotel construction pipeline reached an all-time high of 15,922 projects, with the luxury and upper upscale segments leading growth. The United States leads globally with 6,146 projects (720,089 rooms), representing 39% of the global pipeline. Growth is concentrated in the South and Southwest, with Dallas, Atlanta, and Phoenix as top markets.

Designing The Neuma Brand Identity 

The Neuma Grand is a luxury wellness sanctuary in Arizona, who are positioning themselves as a high-end hospitality destination for high-net worth individuals, looking for a premium experience over existing wellness hotels in Arizona. Neuma needed a new brand identity to justify a high ADR (Average Daily Rate) and drive direct bookings through their website, to rely less on third party travel  sites. Moreover, the scope of this project was to design a logo and brand identity system to achieve better brand recognition and differentiation in the market. A system that can be used with all of Neuma's touchpoints, going forward. 

In this article I describe how I built their brand identity, from sketches to final mock ups. 

First things first! The original name of The Neuma was "The Neuma Collective", which doesn't sound very premium. Therefore, the first thing that was needed is a

more premium name. I changed the name from "The Neuma Collective" to "The Neuma Grand Residency", in accordance with the 5-star rating of the hotel. This immediately changes the brand association / perception to be more luxurious and premium, perfectly in line with their target customer. A "Grand Residency" feels like a second home instead of a hotel.

Research 

Building a strong brand identity is all about doing research and defining a strategy before you start designing. Doing this, the strategy becomes a compass on which the design is built. Without strong research and strategy, design becomes just decoration. To research this project I did a few brand workshops with the customer. 

Keyword workshop (The What)

The goal of this workshop is to come up with a list of 5 keywords that best describe what the company stands for or wants to achieve in the future. After going through a long list of keywords we landed on: transformative, narrative, clinical, immersive and collective. Neuma wants to be seen for the place to go if you want to experience a fully immersive, premium stay where you don't have to think of anything. Staying at The Neuma must be an experience instead of just a "stay". Staying at The Neuma nourishes both the mind and the body, with the highest levels of quality and comfort, all with a clinical, flawless execution. You step into a story and once you leave you can tell your own story.

Target persona workshop (The Who)

After the keyword workshop we did a target persona workshop to decide who the ideal customer of The Neuma is. We landed on mostly high net worth individuals, as can be seen in the image. Just like with the keyword workshop, it's crucial to know who you are designing for, again, otherwise the design becomes just decoration. 

Competitive research workshop

After doing the target persona workshop, it was time for the final workshop, to decide who Neuma's competitors are. We found out that the main competitors are the Civana Wellness Resort and the Sanctuary Camel Back Mountain. Both are beautiful, luxurious wellness resorts with a strong heritage and legacy in the industry. 

At this point I started to get a very clear picture in my head of how we can build a brand identity system that makes The Neuma stand out from their competitors. After all, that's the whole point of branding: to create something identifiable, memorable and hopefully a legacy, but that comes with time. 

Strategy 

After going through all workshops, I found a total of 3 anchors where can differentiate and position The Neuma to comply with the needs from the creative brief and to achieve their strategic goals.

Adding a strong standalone mark, in addition to a wordmark

Most competitors (Civana, Sanctuary Camel Back Mountain), use relatively thin, sans-serif wordmarks that are typical of wellness brands. While they work, all of them look a bit similar. What we need for the Neuma is a strong, identifiable mark, in  addition to a wordmark. The wordmark can be used horizontally on websites and stationary while the mark can be used vertically on signage etc. 

Colours

Most competitors use black on white as their primary colours. While this is "safe" strategy, I think we can differentiate using stronger colour palettes that feel premium and still "breathe" wellness. 

Modern fonts 

When using words like "Grand" and "Residency" in your name, you run the risk of being perceived as old fashioned. To overcome this, we must use a modern sans-serif font that makes the wordmark look modern and contemporary. This also hits home with our target customers. 

Design direction

The direction that I wanted to go with The Neuma is outlined in the image below. This is a design direction based on solid research and strategy, which lends itself perfectly to adaptation and experimentation once we start designing. Going through these workshops has eliminated a lot of variables and unknowns. With that, we are ready to start the design phase. 

Design 

To start the design phase I created a moodboard to get some directions for myself. Again, these are just directions. The idea is to limit yourself to a few directions only. It's easy to get overwhelmed and loose focus. 

Logo sketches 

Sketching on paper is the quickest way to get ideas out of your head, without the complication of using a computer. For this project I started brainstorming and drawing whatever came up. In the end I created approximately 60 sketches. When looking at the keywords I decided to use 2 basic shapes to build the mark with: a circle and a 1/4 circle, to represent Transformation, Immersion, Collective, Clinical, Narrative. 

The Rationale behind my decisions: 

The elements of the sun and the moon represent transformation. The days turns into the night and the night turns into the day in an infinite cycle. This natural rhythm / cadence represents the "let it all go" experience at The Neuma. Every day brings something new and beautiful and has a new story to tell (narrative). At the same time, the moon symbol can also be used as the symbol for the sea if you turn it horizontally. This way it becomes the ultimate metaphor for collective immersion. The circle in the middle represents the sun and it is "hold" by the moons, depicting a sense of collective. The sharp geometric form of the overall sharp indicates the clinical element: the sense of high quality that the Neuma brand stands for.  

I tried many other designs on the computer but I think the moon and sun logo had the most potential. 

Negative shape inside the mark 

Besides being in line with the requirements there are some other reasons why I like this design. The first being that if you look closely, there is a white, negative shape in the mark,  between the moon and the circle. This negative space can be interpreted as the Parvatasana, also known as "Seated Mountain Pose" in Yoga. The circle represents a person while the moon shapes can be interpreted as the hands moving  upwards. This posture promotes deep breathing, it calms the nervous system and improves mental focus. It fits the Neuma brand perfectly. 

Adaptability

A mark should work in all sizes, from very small on a pen, to very big, on top of a building. I think this mark works beautifully in black and white and colour, also if you invert the colours. 

The wordmarks

With the mark ready, it was time for the wordmark. After testing lots of fonts, I settled on a font called "Khand". The strong upward tendency and angular shapes in the letters contrast perfectly with the mark, giving the wordmark a modern, confident and sophisticated look.  

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Adding Colour 

With the mark and wordmark designed and working beautifully in black and white, it was time to add some colours. I designed a primary and secondary colour scheme. 

I wanted to avoid the typical black and gold, "Tiffany" green and other overused colours, still the brand needed to look very sophisticated, with enough colour contrast for the visually impaired. In other words, the colours serve a real purpose. Here is what I landed on: 

Neuma Bordeaux: Melts away external distractions by dropping visual stimulation, triggering a physical response of cozy safety, comfort, and deep immersion. 

Phoenix Ochre: Captures attention rapidly, naturally elevating cognitive alertness, mental energy, and a transformative sense of warmth.

Corvina Champagne: Reflects maximum light to signal cleanliness and openness to the brain, inducing a "clinical" feeling of absolute order, mental clarity, and zero cognitive load.

Creating the pattern 

Every brand needs at least one good pattern to be used as background in their communication.

The pattern I created for The Neuma consists of the two "moons" from the mark, placed on top of each other. By positioning this towards a north-east angle, you get the illusion of waves. These waves move you forward, like every day in The Neuma is new experience. Again, in line with the creative brief. 

Bringing it all together 

With the designs completed it was time for a presentation and mock ups of what we have created!
